segments.title: Why Basse-Terre was the key to Guadeloupe
segments.summary: The British invasion plan concentrates on Basse-Terre and assembles regulars, West India regiments, militia, artillery, and colonial marines without stripping any one garrison.
segments.body: Basse-Terre held the island's main harbor, warehouses, and administrative weight, making it the operational center of gravity. The expedition drew detachments from numerous garrisons so that neighboring islands would not be left exposed, while combining British regulars with locally raised forces.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Basse-Terre as the decisive objective and lists the mixed invasion force.

segments.title: Cochrane's colonial marines and a lost force model
segments.summary: Alexander Cochrane's locally recruited colonial marines became a respected expeditionary force and briefly inspired proposals for mixed Royal Marine brigades.
segments.body: The units were officially wound up after the Caribbean campaigns but personnel and institutional continuity reappeared during the War of 1812. Dr. Clarke highlights a proposal to pair three Royal Marine battalions with a colonial battalion recruited in-theater, an early model for combining permanent and locally raised expeditionary troops.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the colonial marines' continuity, reputation, recruitment, and proposed brigade role.

segments.title: Temeraire-class numbers versus operational results
segments.summary: The Temeraire class supplied much of the battle line at Guadeloupe, prompting a comparison between French standardization and British crewing and rigging practice.
segments.body: France built more than one hundred of these 74-gun ships, while Britain captured twenty-five and used French hull forms as design references. Dr. Clarke questions whether repeating an elegant standard design was enough when doctrine, leadership, and manning did not turn that fleet into victories; British rigging and smaller crews could also translate the same hull volume into greater endurance.

evidence.note: The transcript gives Temeraire-class scale, armament, capture totals, and the crewing and operational-fit comparison.

segments.title: Captured French ships strengthen Cochrane's squadron
segments.summary: HMS Pompée and HMS Abercrombie show how captured French 74s quickly became working Royal Navy capital ships in the Caribbean.
segments.body: Pompée, taken intact after Toulon, served as Cochrane's flagship with a British-modified battery and reduced complement. Abercrombie was captured during a failed French resupply effort, then entered British service quickly enough to take a leading role in the Guadeloupe invasion less than a year later.

evidence.note: The transcript traces Pompée's transfer and armament and Abercrombie's capture and rapid reuse.

segments.title: How Cochrane hid combat power inside transport classifications
segments.summary: Ambiguous accounts of HMS Alfred expose Cochrane's habit of calling partially disarmed warships transports while retaining useful heavy guns.
segments.body: A ship armed en flûte normally surrendered guns for troop capacity, but Dr. Clarke doubts Cochrane removed Alfred's decisive 32- and 18-pounders. The classification let him move extra troops and preserve bombardment strength while presenting the Admiralty with a smaller official combat fleet than he may actually have controlled.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the en-flute ambiguity and Cochrane's administrative method for retaining ships and guns.

segments.title: The landings and outflanking of Basse-Terre
segments.summary: Two British landing columns advance on Basse-Terre while the fleet shifts troops around French positions and naval brigades bring guns ashore.
segments.body: French militia abandoned positions or were bypassed as the British columns converged on the capital. When the French commander moved out to fight the army, Royal Navy and marine forces landed behind him and seized Basse-Terre, turning tactical resistance outside the town into operational defeat.

evidence.note: The transcript narrates the divided landings, coastal maneuver, battle, naval bombardment, and capture of Basse-Terre.

segments.title: Coastal maneuver keeps the fleet inside the land battle
segments.summary: Guadeloupe illustrates how coastal routes, shared intent, and naval mobility can let separate services outflank a defender without constant communications.
segments.body: The army stayed near the coast so ships could support and reposition it, while naval and marine forces exploited the opening at Basse-Terre. Dr. Clarke treats the operation as an example of forces acting with shared purpose: the lack of radio traffic also denied the French warning until landing boats appeared at the objective.

evidence.note: The transcript draws the amphibious lesson from coastal movement, naval support, and coordination without electronic communications.

segments.title: How close did naval guns need to be for shore support?
segments.summary: A viewer asks about 32- and 18-pounder range, clarifying why ships operating a few hundred yards offshore could dominate the objective.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke estimates useful reach at roughly a mile or somewhat more. At Guadeloupe the ships could stand only a few hundred yards offshore, placing shore positions comfortably within their supporting fire envelope.
segments.question: What was the effective range of 32-pounder and 18-pounder naval guns?
segments.answerShort: Roughly a mile or a little more, while the supporting ships at Guadeloupe could operate only a few hundred yards offshore.

evidence.note: The viewer asks for effective range and Dr. Clarke relates it to the close offshore position.

segments.title: Guadeloupe's loss weakens French trade and war finance
segments.summary: The conquest removed a French Caribbean trading base, disrupted slave-trading routes, and redirected colonial wealth toward Britain's wider coalition war.
segments.body: Losing Guadeloupe cut France off from a valuable colony and from a regional transshipment point. British control strengthened its own trade and credit while denying resources to France, helping finance naval expansion and subsidies for continental allies; Cochrane's anti-slavery policy also intersected with recruitment for West India and colonial units.

evidence.note: The transcript describes effects on slave trading, French commerce, British revenue, naval spending, and coalition subsidies.

segments.title: Did France simply regain Guadeloupe in 1814?
segments.summary: A viewer challenges the permanence of conquest, leading to a distinction between territorial restoration and restoration of the old colonial system.
segments.body: France recovered Guadeloupe diplomatically, but Dr. Clarke argues that British occupation had changed its economic and social position, including measures against slavery and the slave trade. Britain also calibrated which colonies to return so the restored Bourbon monarchy looked viable without immediately restoring France's former overseas strength.
segments.question: If France recovered Guadeloupe under the 1814 Treaty of Paris, how lasting was the British victory?
segments.answerShort: France regained the island, but not an unchanged version of its former colonial economy; British occupation and the selective peace settlement left lasting constraints.

evidence.note: The viewer notes the 1814 return and Dr. Clarke explains why political restoration did not fully reverse wartime changes.

segments.title: The West Indies campaign as a model for global war
segments.summary: The neglected West Indies campaign shows how sea control can isolate a peer competitor by stripping away overseas resources and supporting allied fronts.
segments.body: Rather than attacking France only at its strongest point, Britain removed colonial bases and revenue while the Continental System restricted French movement. Dr. Clarke presents this as a durable maritime strategy: immobilize the opponent, secure global supply lines, and dismantle peripheral positions that sustain the main war effort.

evidence.note: The transcript frames the West Indies campaign as a global-war method of removing territories, allies, and resource access.

segments.title: HMS Diamond Rock and the fortified-island warship
segments.summary: A closing aside recalls HMS Diamond Rock, the fortified Caribbean rock that resisted a combined fleet with only a tiny gun detachment.
segments.body: The Royal Navy commissioned a rock as if it were a ship and armed it as a forward position near Martinique. Dr. Clarke points to its prolonged defense against a much larger force as an unusually cinematic example of improvised sea control and coastal denial.

evidence.note: The transcript highlights HMS Diamond Rock's small garrison and resistance to the combined fleet.
